data.description:
Use this tool when you need to ask the user questions during execution. This allows you to:
1. Gather user preferences or requirements
2. Clarify ambiguous instructions
3. Get decisions on implementation choices as you work
4. Offer choices to the user about what direction to take.

Usage notes:
- When `custom` is enabled (default), a "Type your own answer" option is added automatically; don't include "Other" or catch-all options
- Answers are returned as arrays of labels; set `multiple: true` to allow selecting more than one
- If you recommend a specific option, make that the first option in the list and add "(Recommended)" at the end of the label

Executes a given bash command in a persistent shell session with optional timeout, ensuring proper handling and security measures.

Be aware: OS: linux, Shell: bash

All commands run in the current working directory by default. Use the `workdir` parameter if you need to run a command in a different directory. AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>` patterns - use `workdir` instead.

Use `/tmp/opencode` for temporary work outside the workspace. This directory has already been created, already exists, and is pre-approved for external directory access.

IMPORTANT: This tool is for terminal operations like git, npm, docker, etc. DO NOT use it for file operations (reading, writing, editing, searching, finding files) - use the specialized tools for this instead.

Before executing the command, please follow these steps:

1. Directory Verification:
   - If the command will create new directories or files, first use `ls` to verify the parent directory exists and is the correct location
   - For example, before running "mkdir foo/bar", first use `ls foo` to check that "foo" exists and is the intended parent directory

2. Command Execution:
   - Always quote file paths that contain spaces with double quotes (e.g., rm "path with spaces/file.txt")
   - Examples of proper quoting:
     - mkdir "/Users/name/My Documents" (correct)
     - mkdir /Users/name/My Documents (incorrect - will fail)
     - python "/path/with spaces/script.py" (correct)
     - python /path/with spaces/script.py (incorrect - will fail)
   - After ensuring proper quoting, execute the command.
   - Capture the output of the command.

Usage notes:
  - The command argument is required.
  - You can specify an optional timeout in milliseconds. If not specified, commands will time out after 120000ms (2 minutes).
  - It is very helpful if you write a clear, concise description of what this command does in 5-10 words.
  - If the output exceeds 2000 lines or 51200 bytes, it will be truncated and the full output will be written to a file. You can use Read with offset/limit to read specific sections or Grep to search the full content. Do NOT use `head`, `tail`, or other truncation commands to limit output; the full output will already be captured to a file for more precise searching.

  - Avoid using Bash with the `find`, `grep`, `cat`, `head`, `tail`, `sed`, `awk`, or `echo` commands, unless explicitly instructed or when these commands are truly necessary for the task. Instead, always prefer using the dedicated tools for these commands:
    - File search: Use Glob (NOT find or ls)
    - Content search: Use Grep (NOT grep or rg)
    - Read files: Use Read (NOT cat/head/tail)
    - Edit files: Use Edit (NOT sed/awk)
    - Write files: Use Write (NOT echo >/cat <<EOF)
    - Communication: Output text directly (NOT echo/printf)
  - When issuing multiple commands:
    - If the commands are independent and can run in parallel, make multiple Bash tool calls in a single message. For example, if you need to run "git status" and "git diff", send a single message with two Bash tool calls in parallel.
    - If the commands depend on each other and must run sequentially, use a single Bash call with '&&' to chain them together (e.g., `git add . && git commit -m "message" && git push`). For instance, if one operation must complete before another starts (like mkdir before cp, Write before Bash for git operations, or git add before git commit), run these operations sequentially instead.
    - Use ';' only when you need to run commands sequentially but don't care if earlier commands fail
    - DO NOT use newlines to separate commands (newlines are ok in quoted strings)
  - AVOID using `cd <directory> && <command>`. Use the `workdir` parameter to change directories instead.
    <good-example>
    Use workdir="/foo/bar" with command: pytest tests
    </good-example>
    <bad-example>
    cd /foo/bar && pytest tests
    </bad-example>

# Committing changes with git

Only create commits when requested by the user. If unclear, ask first. When the user asks you to create a new git commit, follow these steps carefully:

Git Safety Protocol:
- NEVER update the git config
- NEVER run destructive/irreversible git commands (like push --force, hard reset, etc) unless the user explicitly requests them
- NEVER skip hooks (--no-verify, --no-gpg-sign, etc) unless the user explicitly requests it
- NEVER run force push to main/master, warn the user if they request it
- Avoid git commit --amend. ONLY use --amend when ALL conditions are met:
  (1) User explicitly requested amend, OR the commit succeeded and pre-commit hooks auto-modified files that need including — verify by checking `git log` that HEAD is the new commit before amending
  (2) HEAD commit was created by you in this conversation (verify: git log -1 --format='%an %ae')
  (3) Commit has NOT been pushed to remote (verify: git status shows "Your branch is ahead")
- CRITICAL: If commit FAILED or was REJECTED by hook, NEVER amend - fix the issue and create a NEW commit
- CRITICAL: If you already pushed to remote, NEVER amend unless user explicitly requests it (requires force push)
- NEVER commit changes unless the user explicitly asks you to. It is VERY IMPORTANT to only commit when explicitly asked, otherwise the user will feel that you are being too proactive.

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel, each using the Bash tool:
  - Run a git status command to see all untracked files.
  - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed.
  - Run a git log command to see recent commit messages, so that you can follow this repository's commit message style.
2. Analyze all staged changes (both previously staged and newly added) and draft a commit message:
  - Summarize the nature of the changes (eg. new feature, enhancement to an existing feature, bug fix, refactoring, test, docs, etc.). Ensure the message accurately reflects the changes and their purpose (i.e. "add" means a wholly new feature, "update" means an enhancement to an existing feature, "fix" means a bug fix, etc.).
  - Do not commit files that likely contain secrets (.env, credentials.json, etc.). Warn the user if they specifically request to commit those files
  - Draft a concise (1-2 sentences) commit message that focuses on the "why" rather than the "what"
  - Ensure it accurately reflects the changes and their purpose
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands:
   - Add relevant untracked files to the staging area.
   - Create the commit with a message
   - Run git status after the commit completes to verify success.
   Note: git status depends on the commit completing, so run it sequentially after the commit.
4. If the commit fails due to pre-commit hook, fix the issue and create a NEW commit (see amend rules above)

Important notes:
- NEVER run additional commands to read or explore code, besides git bash commands
- NEVER use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- DO NOT push to the remote repository unless the user explicitly asks you to do so
- IMPORTANT: Never use git commands with the -i flag (like git rebase -i or git add -i) since they require interactive input which is not supported.
- If there are no changes to commit (i.e., no untracked files and no modifications), do not create an empty commit

# Creating pull requests
Use the gh command via the Bash tool for ALL GitHub-related tasks including working with issues, pull requests, checks, and releases. If given a GitHub URL use the gh command to get the information needed.

IMPORTANT: When the user asks you to create a pull request, follow these steps carefully:

1. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following bash commands in parallel using the Bash tool, in order to understand the current state of the branch since it diverged from the main branch:
   - Run a git status command to see all untracked files
   - Run a git diff command to see both staged and unstaged changes that will be committed
   - Check if the current branch tracks a remote branch and is up to date with the remote, so you know if you need to push to the remote
   - Run a git log command and `git diff [base-branch]...HEAD` to understand the full commit history for the current branch (from the time it diverged from the base branch)
2. Analyze all changes that will be included in the pull request, making sure to look at all relevant commits (NOT just the latest commit, but ALL commits that will be included in the pull request!!!), and draft a pull request summary
3. You can call multiple tools in a single response. When multiple independent pieces of information are requested and all commands are likely to succeed, run multiple tool calls in parallel for optimal performance. run the following commands in parallel:
   - Create new branch if needed
   - Push to remote with -u flag if needed
   - Create PR using gh pr create with the format below. Use a HEREDOC to pass the body to ensure correct formatting.
<example>
gh pr create --title "the pr title" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary
<1-3 bullet points>
</example>

Important:
- DO NOT use the TodoWrite or Task tools
- Return the PR URL when you're done, so the user can see it

# Other common operations
- View comments on a GitHub PR: gh api repos/foo/bar/pulls/123/comments

data.description:
Read a file or directory from the local filesystem. If the path does not exist, an error is returned.

Usage:
- The filePath parameter should be an absolute path.
- By default, this tool returns up to 2000 lines from the start of the file.
- The offset parameter is the line number to start from (1-indexed).
- To read later sections, call this tool again with a larger offset.
- Use the grep tool to find specific content in large files or files with long lines.
- If you are unsure of the correct file path, use the glob tool to look up filenames by glob pattern.
- Contents are returned with each line prefixed by its line number as `<line>: <content>`. For example, if a file has contents "foo\n", you will receive "1: foo\n". For directories, entries are returned one per line (without line numbers) with a trailing `/` for subdirectories.
- Any line longer than 2000 characters is truncated.
- Call this tool in parallel when you know there are multiple files you want to read.
- Avoid tiny repeated slices (30 line chunks). If you need more context, read a larger window.
- This tool can read image files and PDFs and return them as file attachments.

filePath.description: The absolute path to the file or directory to read

offset.description: The line number to start reading from (1-indexed)

limit.description: The maximum number of lines to read (defaults to 2000)

data.description:
- Fast file pattern matching tool that works with any codebase size
- Supports glob patterns like "**/*.js" or "src/**/*.ts"
- Returns matching file paths sorted by modification time
- Use this tool when you need to find files by name patterns
- When you are doing an open-ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the Task tool instead
- You have the capability to call multiple tools in a single response. It is always better to speculatively perform multiple searches as a batch that are potentially useful.

pattern.description: The glob pattern to match files against

path.description: The directory to search in. If not specified, the current working directory will be used. IMPORTANT: Omit this field to use the default directory. DO NOT enter "undefined" or "null" - simply omit it for the default behavior. Must be a valid directory path if provided.

data.description:
- Fast content search tool that works with any codebase size
- Searches file contents using regular expressions
- Supports full regex syntax (eg. "log.*Error", "function\s+\w+", etc.)
- Filter files by pattern with the include parameter (eg. "*.js", "*.{ts,tsx}")
- Returns file paths and line numbers with at least one match sorted by modification time
- Use this tool when you need to find files containing specific patterns
- If you need to identify/count the number of matches within files, use the Bash tool with `rg` (ripgrep) directly. Do NOT use `grep`.
- When you are doing an open-ended search that may require multiple rounds of globbing and grepping, use the Task tool instead

pattern.description: The regex pattern to search for in file contents

path.description: The directory to search in. Defaults to the current working directory.

include.description: File pattern to include in the search (e.g. "*.js", "*.{ts,tsx}")

data.description:
Performs exact string replacements in files. 

Usage:
- You must use your `Read` tool at least once in the conversation before editing. This tool will error if you attempt an edit without reading the file. 
- When editing text from Read tool output, ensure you preserve the exact indentation (tabs/spaces) as it appears AFTER the line number prefix. The line number prefix format is: line number + colon + space (e.g., `1: `). Everything after that space is the actual file content to match. Never include any part of the line number prefix in the oldString or newString.
- ALWAYS prefer editing existing files in the codebase. NEVER write new files unless explicitly required.
- Only use emojis if the user explicitly requests it. Avoid adding emojis to files unless asked.
- The edit will FAIL if `oldString` is not found in the file with an error "oldString not found in content".
- The edit will FAIL if `oldString` is found multiple times in the file with an error "Found multiple matches for oldString. Provide more surrounding lines in oldString to identify the correct match." Either provide a larger string with more surrounding context to make it unique or use `replaceAll` to change every instance of `oldString`. 
- Use `replaceAll` for replacing and renaming strings across the file. This parameter is useful if you want to rename a variable for instance.

filePath.description: The absolute path to the file to modify

newString.description: The text to replace it with (must be different from oldString)

replaceAll.description: Replace all occurrences of oldString (default false)

data.description: Launch a new agent to handle complex, multistep tasks autonomously. When using the Task tool, you must specify a subagent_type parameter to select which agent type to use. When to use the Task tool: - When you are instructed to execute custom slash commands. Use the Task tool with the slash command invocation as the entire prompt. The slash command can take arguments. For example: Task(description="Check the file", prompt="/check-file path/to/file.py") When NOT to use the Task tool: - If you want to read a specific file path, use the Read or Glob tool instead of the Task tool, to find the match more quickly - If you are searching for a specific class definition like "class Foo", use the Glob tool instead, to find the match more quickly - If you are searching for code within a specific file or set of 2-3 files, use the Read tool instead of the Task tool, to find the match more quickly - Other tasks that are not related to the agent descriptions above Usage notes: 1. Launch multiple agents concurrently whenever possible, to maximize performance; to do that, use a single message with multiple tool uses 2. When the agent is done, it will return a single message back to you. The result returned by the agent is not visible to the user. To show the user the result, you should send a text message back to the user with a concise summary of the result. The output includes a task_id you can reuse later to continue the same subagent session. 3. Each agent invocation starts with a fresh context unless you provide task_id to resume the same subagent session (which continues with its previous messages and tool outputs). When starting fresh, your prompt should contain a highly detailed task description for the agent to perform autonomously and you should specify exactly what information the agent should return back to you in its final and only message to you. 4. The agent's outputs should generally be trusted 5. Clearly tell the agent whether you expect it to write code or just to do research (search, file reads, web fetches, etc.), since it is not aware of the user's intent. Tell it how to verify its work if possible (e.g., relevant test commands). 6. If the agent description mentions that it should be used proactively, then you should try your best to use it without the user having to ask for it first. Use your judgement. Example usage (NOTE: The agents below are fictional examples for illustration only - use the actual agents listed above): "code-reviewer": use this agent after you are done writing a significant piece of code "greeting-responder": use this agent when to respond to user greetings with a friendly joke user: "Please write a function that checks if a number is prime" assistant: Sure let me write a function that checks if a number is prime assistant: First let me use the Write tool to write a function that checks if a number is prime assistant: I'm going to use the Write tool to write the following code: function isPrime(n) { if (n <= 1) return false for (let i = 2; i * i <= n; i++) { if (n % i === 0) return false } return true } Since a significant piece of code was written and the task was completed, now use the code-reviewer agent to review the code assistant: Now let me use the code-reviewer agent to review the code assistant: Uses the Task tool to launch the code-reviewer agent user: "Hello" Since the user is greeting, use the greeting-responder agent to respond with a friendly joke assistant: "I'm going to use the Task tool to launch the with the greeting-responder agent" Available agent types and the tools they have access to:

description.description: A short (3-5 words) description of the task

prompt.description: The task for the agent to perform

subagent_type.description: The type of specialized agent to use for this task

task_id.description: This should only be set if you mean to resume a previous task (you can pass a prior task_id and the task will continue the same subagent session as before instead of creating a fresh one)

command.description: The command that triggered this task

data.description:
Use this tool to create and manage a structured task list for your current coding session. This helps you track progress, organize complex tasks, and demonstrate thoroughness to the user.
It also helps the user understand the progress of the task and overall progress of their requests.

## When to Use This Tool
Use this tool proactively in these scenarios:

1. Complex multistep tasks - When a task requires 3 or more distinct steps or actions
2. Non-trivial and complex tasks - Tasks that require careful planning or multiple operations
3. User explicitly requests todo list - When the user directly asks you to use the todo list
4. User provides multiple tasks - When users provide a list of things to be done (numbered or comma-separated)
5. After receiving new instructions - Immediately capture user requirements as todos. Feel free to edit the todo list based on new information.
6. After completing a task - Mark it complete and add any new follow-up tasks
7. When you start working on a new task, mark the todo as in_progress. Ideally you should only have one todo as in_progress at a time. Complete existing tasks before starting new ones.

## When NOT to Use This Tool

Skip using this tool when:
1. There is only a single, straightforward task
2. The task is trivial and tracking it provides no organizational benefit
3. The task can be completed in less than 3 trivial steps
4. The task is purely conversational or informational

NOTE that you should not use this tool if there is only one trivial task to do. In this case you are better off just doing the task directly.

## Examples of When to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: I want to add a dark mode toggle to the application settings. Make sure you run the tests and build when you're done!
Assistant: I'll help add a dark mode toggle to your application settings. Let me create a todo list to track this implementation.
*Creates todo list with the following items:*
1. Create dark mode toggle component in Settings page
2. Add dark mode state management (context/store)
3. Implement CSS-in-JS styles for dark theme
4. Update existing components to support theme switching
5. Run tests and build process, addressing any failures or errors that occur
*Begins working on the first task*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. Adding dark mode is a multi-step feature requiring UI, state management, and styling changes
2. The user explicitly requested tests and build be run afterward
3. The assistant inferred that tests and build need to pass by adding "Ensure tests and build succeed" as the final task
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Help me rename the function getCwd to getCurrentWorkingDirectory across my project
Assistant: Let me first search through your codebase to find all occurrences of 'getCwd'.
*Uses grep or search tools to locate all instances of getCwd in the codebase*
Assistant: I've found 15 instances of 'getCwd' across 8 different files. Let me create a todo list to track these changes.
*Creates todo list with specific items for each file that needs updating*

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant searched to understand the scope of the task
2. Upon finding multiple occurrences across different files, it determined this was a complex task with multiple steps
3. The todo list helps ensure every instance is tracked and updated systematically
4. This approach prevents missing any occurrences and maintains code consistency
</reasoning>
</example>


<example>
User: I need to implement these features for my e-commerce site: user registration, product catalog, shopping cart, and checkout flow.
Assistant: I'll help implement these features. First, let's add all the features to the todo list.
*Creates a todo list breaking down each feature into specific tasks based on the project architecture*
Assistant: Let's start with user registration. This will involve creating database models, API endpoints, and frontend forms that integrate with your existing authentication system.

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. The user provided multiple complex features to implement in a comma separated list
2. The todo list helps organize these large features into manageable tasks
3. This approach allows for tracking progress across the entire implementation
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you help optimize my React application? It's rendering slowly and has performance issues.</user>
Assistant: I'll help optimize your React application. First, let me examine your codebase to identify potential performance bottlenecks.
*Reviews component structure, render patterns, state management, and data fetching*
Assistant: After analyzing your codebase, I've identified several performance issues. Let me create a todo list to track our optimization efforts.
*Creates todo list with items like: 1) Implement memoization for expensive calculations in ProductList, 2) Add virtualization for long lists in Dashboard, 3) Optimize image loading in Gallery component, 4) Fix state update loops in ShoppingCart, 5) Review bundle size and implement code splitting*
Let's start by implementing memoization for the expensive calculations in your ProductList component.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant used the todo list because:
1. First, the assistant examined the codebase to identify specific performance issues
2. Based on this analysis, it identified multiple optimization opportunities
3. Performance optimization is a non-trivial task requiring multiple steps
4. The todo list helps methodically track improvements across different components
5. This systematic approach ensures all performance bottlenecks are addressed
</reasoning>
</example>

## Examples of When NOT to Use the Todo List

<example>
User: How do I print 'Hello World' in Python?
Assistant: In Python, you can print "Hello World" with this simple code:

python
print("Hello World")

This will output the text "Hello World" to the console when executed.</assistant>

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, trivial task that can be completed in one step. There's no need to track multiple tasks or steps for such a straightforward request.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: What does the git status command do?
Assistant: The git status command shows the current state of your working directory and staging area. It displays which changes have been staged, which haven't, and which files aren't being tracked by Git. This is a fundamental command for understanding the current state of your repository before making commits.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is an informational request with no actual coding task to complete. The user is simply asking for an explanation, not for the assistant to perform multiple steps or tasks.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Can you add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does?
Assistant: Sure, let me add a comment to the calculateTotal function to explain what it does.
* Uses the Edit tool to add a comment to the calculateTotal function *

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single, straightforward task confined to one location in the code. Adding a comment doesn't require tracking multiple steps or systematic organization.
</reasoning>
</example>

<example>
User: Run npm install for me and tell me what happens.
Assistant: I'll run the npm install command for you.

*Executes: npm install*

The command completed successfully. Here's the output:
[Output of npm install command]

All dependencies have been installed according to your package.json file.

<reasoning>
The assistant did not use the todo list because this is a single command execution with immediate results. There are no multiple steps to track or organize, making the todo list unnecessary for this straightforward task.
</reasoning>
</example>

## Task States and Management

1. **Task States**: Use these states to track progress:
   - pending: Task not yet started
   - in_progress: Currently working on (limit to ONE task at a time)
   - completed: Task finished successfully
   - cancelled: Task no longer needed

2. **Task Management**:
   - Update task status in real-time as you work
   - Mark tasks complete IMMEDIATELY after finishing (don't batch completions)
   - Only have ONE task in_progress at any time
   - Complete current tasks before starting new ones
   - Cancel tasks that become irrelevant

3. **Task Breakdown**:
   - Create specific, actionable items
   - Break complex tasks into smaller, manageable steps
   - Use clear, descriptive task names

When in doubt, use this tool. Being proactive with task management demonstrates attentiveness and ensures you complete all requirements successfully.

status.description: Current status of the task: pending, in_progress, completed, cancelled

priority.description: Priority level of the task: high, medium, low

data.name: write-cypher
data.description: Use when working with VAST DB to write Cypher queries for code analysis. Covers query patterns for finding operations by source location, finding an operation's enclosing function, locating symbols, exploring call relationships, and tracing def-use data flow. Load understand-mlir-schema when schema details are needed to construct queries or interpret query results.
data.content:
# Write Cypher

## Overview

Use this skill for VAST DB Cypher query patterns. Prefer reusable templates, and construct new queries when no template matches the analysis need.

## Workflow

1. Read `references/query-templates.md`. If a template matches the task, reuse it as-is.
2. If no template matches, construct a new Cypher query using the VAST DB schema and the closest related template as guidance.

## Query Strategy

- Load `understand-mlir-schema` when query construction or result interpretation depends on schema details.
- Use small exploratory queries with `LIMIT` when schema details such as operation names, properties, node and relationship labels are uncertain.
- When starting from a source location, first map it to operation `uid`s. Use those `uid`s as stable handles for follow-up queries such as enclosing functions, call relationships, and def-use data flow.
- Return only properties needed for the analysis. Common fields are node `uid`, relationship `idx`, operation `name`, `location`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `className`, type `type`, and attribute `value`.
- Order indexed relationships by `idx`.

## Query Rules

- Generate read-only queries by default. Do not use `CREATE`, `MERGE`, `SET`, `DELETE`, or `REMOVE`.
- Use parameter placeholders such as `$func_name`, `$op_uid`, `$file_name`, `$line`, and `$callee`.
- Do not invent node and relationship labels, properties, or operation names. Verify unfamiliar schema details with `understand-mlir-schema` or exploratory queries.

## Common Patterns

Read `references/query-templates.md` for reusable Cypher templates.

data.name: understand-mlir-schema
data.description: Use to understand the VAST DB schema: what node labels exist (OPERATIONS, VALUES, TYPES, etc.), how nodes connect through relationships (RESVALUE, ARGVALUE, CHILDREGION, etc.), and what properties each node kind carries. Use this before writing Cypher queries against VAST DB. For query patterns, load write-cypher.
data.content:
# Understand MLIR Schema

## Overview

This skill describes the VAST DB schema. Use it to check node labels, relationship types, and properties before writing or interpreting Cypher queries. Do not guess labels or relationships; look them up here.

## Core Schema

### Node Labels

- `OPERATIONS` — MLIR operations. Properties include `uid`, `name`, `func_name`, `fs_raw_name`, `varName`, `mod_name`, and `location`. The `location` field has the form `['F', file, line, column]`.
- `VALUES` — SSA values. Only property is `uid`.
- `INTATTRS` — Integer attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (int), `attr_type` (int).
- `FLOATATTRS` — Float attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (float), `attr_type` (int).
- `STRINGATTRS` — String attributes. Properties: `uid`, `value` (string), `attr_type` (int).
- `TYPES` — MLIR types. Properties: `uid`, `type` (type mnemonic), `has_dl` (bool), `bw` (int bitwidth), `align` (int), `record_name` (string, for hl.record only).
- `REGIONS` — MLIR regions. Properties: `uid`, `regType` (bool: true for GraphRegion, false for SSACFG).
- `BLOCKS` — MLIR blocks. Only property is `uid`.
- `VTABLES` — Virtual tables. Properties: `uid`, `vtable_name`, `components_flat`, `components_sizes`.
- `VTTS` — VTT (virtual table table). Properties: `uid`, `vtt_name`, `components_flat`.

### Key Relationships

Operation operands and results:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation consumes a value as operand
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:RESVALUE {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — operation produces a value as result
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGATTR {idx}]->(:INTATTRS|FLOATATTRS|STRINGATTRS)` — operation has an attribute
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — operation references a block (e.g., branch targets)
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGTYPE {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — operation has a type operand

Containment (operation bodies):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:CHILDREGION {idx}]->(:REGIONS)` — operation contains a region
- `(:REGIONS)-[:CHILDBLOCK {idx}]->(:BLOCKS)` — region contains a block
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:CHILDINST {idx}]->(:OPERATIONS)` — block contains an instruction (operation)
- `(:BLOCKS)-[:BLOCKARG {idx}]->(:VALUES)` — block has a block argument

Type system:
- `(:VALUES)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — value has a type
- `(:TYPES)-[:ARG {idx}]->(:TYPES)` — composite type has component types
- `(:INTATTRS|:FLOATATTRS)-[:TYPE]->(:TYPES)` — non-string attribute has a type

Function and variable types:
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:FUNCTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global function's type signature
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:VARTYPE]->(:TYPES)` — global variable's type

Virtual tables (only from `builtin.module` operations):
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTABLE]->(:VTABLES)` — module has a vtable
- `(:OPERATIONS)-[:ARGVTT]->(:VTTS)` — module has a VTT

## References

- `references/schema-mlir-graph.md` — canonical node labels, relationships, properties, constraints, and example queries.
- `references/operations-catalog.md` — every operation name (`hl.func`, `hl.call`, `ll.load`, etc.) with its specific properties and relationships.
- `references/types-catalog.md` — every type mnemonic (`hl.int`, `i32`, `core.fn`, etc.) with its properties and subtype structure.

Notes gf_free(iod) not in test case. Misses the precise freeing context: descriptors are freed in xmt_node_end when 'outside scene scope' while od_links retains dangling l->od references.

parts.text:
Now I have a clear picture of the vulnerability. Let me look at the key difference between bad.c and good.c.

In `bad.c`, the `xmt_node_end` function has:
```c
else if (!parser->od_command) {
    xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: descriptor %s defined outside scene scope - skipping", name);
    gf_odf_desc_del(desc);
}
```

In `good.c`, there's a new function `xmt_remove_link_for_descriptor` that's called before `gf_odf_desc_del(desc)`:
```c
else if (!parser->od_command) {
    xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: descriptor %s defined outside scene scope - skipping", name);
    xmt_remove_link_for_descriptor(parser, desc);
    gf_odf_desc_del(desc);
}
```

The `xmt_remove_link_for_descriptor` function (lines 695-736 in good.c) iterates through `parser->od_links`, finds the entry whose `l->od` points to the descriptor being freed, sets `l->od = NULL`, removes the link from the list, and frees the `XMT_ODLink` structure.

This prevents the use-after-free because later in `xmt_resolve_od_links` (line 1644), the check `l->od && !l->od->objectDescriptorID` will correctly handle the case where `l->od` has been set to NULL.

Now, let me evaluate both explanations:

**Baseline explanation:**
- Says correctly it's a UAF in the XMT parser
- Points to the sink at line 1644: `l->od->objectDescriptorID`
- Points to the source at line 588: `gf_odf_del_iod` (called via `gf_odf_delete_descriptor`)
- Notes the source `gf_free(iod)` is not explicitly in this test case
- The trigger path: `load_xmt_run` → `xmt_resolve_od_links` → sink at line 1644
- Mentions that descriptors are stored in `parser->od_links` list

The baseline correctly identifies:
1. The UAF vulnerability type
2. The sink location (line 1644)
3. The source (free via `gf_odf_del_iod` at line 588)
4. The data flow: od_links hold dangling pointers after iod is freed

However, the baseline doesn't identify the critical trigger mechanism: the descriptor is freed in `xmt_node_end` when it's "defined outside scene scope" (the `!parser->od_command` path), and the dangling `l->od` pointer in `od_links` is later accessed in `xmt_resolve_od_links`. The baseline mentions "descriptors are stored in `parser->od_links` list" but the descriptors themselves are NOT stored in od_links - the od_links store XMT_ODLink structures that each have an `od` pointer to a descriptor. The link is between the `l->od` pointer and the freed `iod`.

The baseline is mostly correct but misses the precise trigger mechanism - that the free happens specifically in `xmt_node_end` when a descriptor is outside scene scope.

**VASTDB explanation:**
- Also correctly identifies UAF (CWE-416)
- Points to `gf_odf_del_iod` freeing `iod` at line 342 (declaration) / line 588 (call)
- Points to sink at line 1644
- Trigger path: `load_xmt_run` → `xmt_resolve_od_links` at line 1546 → sink at line 1644
- Mentions that `l->od` and `iod` may point to the same object

The vastdb explanation is very similar to the baseline. It also doesn't identify the key mechanism where the free is triggered in `xmt_node_end` when descriptors are outside scene scope. It also notes the VAST DB doesn't contain relevant data for this C test case.

Both explanations are reasonable but miss the critical detail of how exactly the descriptors get freed before `xmt_resolve_od_links` runs. The free path is:
1. XML parsing happens, which creates descriptors 
2. Some descriptors are created "outside scene scope" (no `parser->od_command` is set)
3. In `xmt_node_end`, these descriptors are freed via `gf_odf_desc_del(desc)` → `gf_odf_delete_descriptor` → `gf_odf_del_iod`
4. But `od_links` still contains `XMT_ODLink` entries whose `l->od` pointer points to the same descriptor
5. When `xmt_resolve_od_links` runs, it accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID` on the freed memory

Both explanations correctly identify the UAF, the source (gf_free/gf_odf_del_iod), and the sink (l->od->objectDescriptorID). Neither goes into the precise intermediate data-flow detail of how the link between the descriptor and OD link is established, but both give a reasonable analysis.

Let me compare the scores:

Both are fairly similar. The baseline is slightly more specific about pointing out that `gf_free(iod)` is not in the test case (showing understanding of the test case boundaries). Both correctly identify the vulnerability type, source, sink, and main trigger path.

I'd give both a score around 7-8. They correctly identify the root cause (UAF), source and sink locations, and the main call chain. However, neither identifies the precise intermediate flow: that the key is the `xmt_node_end` function freeing descriptors that are "outside scene scope" while the `od_links` list retains dangling references. This omission is important for understanding the complete trigger mechanism.

Let me set scores and justifications.

1: Looking at the code, I can now provide the analysis:

3: ## Root Cause
4: 
5: The vulnerability is a Use-After-Free in the XMT (XML-based MPEG-4 text format) parser. The `XMT_ODLink` structure (line 200) contains a pointer `OD *od` to an Object Descriptor. During parsing, descriptors are stored in `parser->od_links` list. The sink at line 1644 accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID` without verifying the memory is still valid. If the underlying `GF_InitialObjectDescriptor` (iod) is freed (via `gf_odf_del_iod` at line 588) while references in `od_links` still point to freed memory, the subsequent access to `l->od->objectDescriptorID` dereferences freed memory.
6:

13:    - Iterates through parser->od_links and accesses l->od->objectDescriptorID at the sink
14: 
15: 3. `bad.c:1644` sink location
16:    - Accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID` - vulnerable use-after-free location
17:

In the full GPAC codebase, the free would occur in `gf_odf_delete_descriptor` -> `gf_odf_del_iod` (line 588), which frees the InitialObjectDescriptor while `od_links` may still hold dangling pointers to the freed memory.

(End of file - total 18 lines)

metadata.preview:
Looking at the code, I can now provide the analysis:

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a Use-After-Free in the XMT (XML-based MPEG-4 text format) parser. The `XMT_ODLink` structure (line 200) contains a pointer `OD *od` to an Object Descriptor. During parsing, descriptors are stored in `parser->od_links` list. The sink at line 1644 accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID` without verifying the memory is still valid. If the underlying `GF_InitialObjectDescriptor` (iod) is freed (via `gf_odf_del_iod` at line 588) while references in `od_links` still point to freed memory, the subsequent access to `l->od->objectDescriptorID` dereferences freed memory.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:1716` `load_xmt_run`
   - Entry point for XMT scene loading, sets up the parser and calls xmt_resolve_od_links

2. `bad.c:1731` `xmt_resolve_od_links` (called from load_xmt_run)
   - Iterates through parser->od_links and accesses l->od->objectDescriptorID at the sink

3. `bad.c:1644` sink location
   - Accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID` - vulnerable use-after-free location

Note: The source `gf_free(iod)` is not explicitly present in this reduced test case file. In the full GPAC codebase, the free would occur in `gf_odf_delete_descriptor` -> `gf_odf_del_iod` (line 588), which frees the InitialObjectDescriptor while `od_links` may still hold dangling pointers to the freed memory.

metadata.preview:
Based on my analysis of the code, the VAST DB contains MLIR data from the repository but not the specific C test case. The queries don't return relevant results for this C code. I'll provide the analysis based on code inspection.

## Root Cause

The vulnerability is a Use-After-Free (CWE-416). The `gf_odf_del_iod` function (declared at line 342, called at line 588) frees the `GF_InitialObjectDescriptor *iod` structure. If `l->od` (from the `XMT_ODLink` structure at line 200) points to the same memory as `iod`, after `gf_free(iod)` is called, `l->od` becomes a dangling pointer. When the code at line 1644 accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID`, it dereferences freed memory, triggering the use-after-free.

## Trigger Path

1. `bad.c:1716` `load_xmt_run`
   - Entry point function that loads XMT scene

2. `bad.c:1731` calls `xmt_resolve_od_links(parser)`
   - Called after XML parsing completes to resolve OD links

3. `bad.c:1546` `xmt_resolve_od_links`
   - Iterates through `parser->od_links` list to process `XMT_ODLink` entries

4. `bad.c:1644` `if (l->od && !l->od->objectDescriptorID)`
   - Sink: Accesses `l->od->objectDescriptorID` which may be freed memory if `l->od` references the same object as `iod`

486: static Bool xmt_esid_available(GF_XMTParser *parser, u16 ESID) {
487: 	u32 i; XMT_ESDLink *esdl;
488: 	i=0;
489: 	while ((esdl = (XMT_ESDLink *)gf_list_enum(parser->esd_links, &i))) {
490: 		if (esdl->ESID == ESID) return 0;
491: 	}
492: 	return 1;
493: }
494: 
495: static Bool xmt_odid_available(GF_XMTParser *parser, u16 ODID) {
496: 	return 1;
497: }

499: GF_EXPORT
500: GF_SAXParser *gf_xml_sax_new(gf_xml_sax_node_start on_node_start,
501:                               gf_xml_sax_node_end on_node_end,
502:                               gf_xml_sax_text_content on_text_content,
503:                               void *cbck)
504: {
505: 	GF_SAXParser *parser;
506: 	GF_SAFEALLOC(parser, GF_SAXParser);
507: 	if (!parser) return NULL;
508: 	parser->entities = gf_list_new();
509: 	parser->unicode_type = -1;
510: 	parser->sax_node_start = on_node_start;
511: 	parser->sax_node_end = on_node_end;
512: 	parser->sax_text_content = on_text_content;
513: 	parser->sax_cbck = cbck;
514: 	return parser;
515: }
516: 
517: static GF_XMTParser *xmt_new_parser(GF_SceneLoader *load)
518: {
519: 	GF_XMTParser *parser;
520: 	if ((load->type==GF_SM_LOAD_XSR) && !load->ctx) return NULL;
521: 	GF_SAFEALLOC(parser, GF_XMTParser);
522: 	if (!parser) return NULL;
523: 
524: 	parser->nodes = gf_list_new();
525: 	parser->descriptors = gf_list_new();
526: 	parser->od_links = gf_list_new();
527: 	parser->esd_links = gf_list_new();
528: 	parser->def_nodes = gf_list_new();
529: 	parser->peeked_nodes = gf_list_new();
530: 	parser->inserted_routes = gf_list_new();
531: 	parser->unresolved_routes = gf_list_new();
532: 
533: 	parser->sax_parser = gf_xml_sax_new(xmt_node_start, xmt_node_end, xmt_text_content, parser);
534: 	parser->load = load;
535: 	load->loader_priv = parser;
536: 	if (load->ctx) load->ctx->is_pixel_metrics = 1;
537: 
538: 	return parser;
539: }
540: 
541: static GF_Err load_xmt_initialize(GF_SceneLoader *load, const char *str_data)
542: {
543: 	GF_Err e;
544: 	GF_XMTParser *parser;
545: 
546: 	if (str_data) {
547: 		char BOM[5];
548: 		if (strlen(str_data)<4) return GF_BAD_PARAM;
549: 		BOM[0] = str_data[0];
550: 		BOM[1] = str_data[1];
551: 		BOM[2] = str_data[2];
552: 		BOM[3] = str_data[3];
553: 		BOM[4] = 0;
554: 		parser = xmt_new_parser(load);
555: 		e = gf_xml_sax_init(parser->sax_parser, (unsigned char*)BOM);
556: 		if (e) {
557: 			xmt_report(parser, e, "Error initializing SAX parser");
558: 			return e;
559: 		}
560: 		str_data += 4;
561: 	} else if (load->fileName) {
562: 		parser = xmt_new_parser(load);
563: 	} else {
564: 		return GF_BAD_PARAM;
565: 	}
566: 
567: 	/*chunk parsing*/
568: 	if (load->flags & GF_SM_LOAD_CONTEXT_READY) {
569: 		GF_LOG(GF_LOG_INFO, GF_LOG_PARSER, ("XMT: MPEG-4 (XMT) Chunk Parsing\n"));
570: 
571: 		e = xmt_restore_context(load);
572: 		if (e) return e;
573: 
574: 	} else {
575: 		GF_LOG(GF_LOG_INFO, GF_LOG_PARSER, ("XMT: MPEG-4 (XMT) Scene Parsing\n"));
576: 	}
577: 
578: 	if (str_data) {
579: 		return gf_xml_sax_parse(parser->sax_parser, str_data);
580: 	}
581: 	return GF_OK;
582: }
583: 
584: GF_Err gf_odf_delete_descriptor(GF_Descriptor *desc)
585: {
586: 	switch (desc->tag) {
587: 	case GF_ODF_IOD_TAG :
588: 		return gf_odf_del_iod((GF_InitialObjectDescriptor *)desc);
589: 	case GF_ODF_OD_TAG:
590: 		return gf_odf_del_od((GF_ObjectDescriptor *)desc);
591: 	case GF_ODF_ESD_TAG :
592: 		return gf_odf_del_esd((GF_ESD *)desc);
593: 	case GF_ODF_DCD_TAG :
594: 		return gf_odf_del_dcd((GF_DecoderConfig *)desc);
595: 	case GF_ODF_SLC_TAG:
596: 		return gf_odf_del_slc((GF_SLConfig *)desc);
597: 
598: 	case GF_ODF_ISOM_IOD_TAG:
599: 		return gf_odf_del_isom_iod((GF_IsomInitialObjectDescriptor *)desc);
600: 	case GF_ODF_ISOM_OD_TAG:
601: 		return gf_odf_del_isom_od((GF_IsomObjectDescriptor *)desc);
602: 
603: 	case GF_ODF_SEGMENT_TAG:
604: 		return gf_odf_del_segment((void *) desc);
605: 
606: 	case GF_ODF_MUXINFO_TAG:
607: 		return gf_odf_del_muxinfo((void *)desc);
608: 	case GF_ODF_BIFS_CFG_TAG:
609: 		return gf_odf_del_bifs_cfg((void *)desc);
610: 	case GF_ODF_UI_CFG_TAG:
611: 		return gf_odf_del_ui_cfg((void *)desc);
612: 	case GF_ODF_TEXT_CFG_TAG:
613: 		return gf_odf_del_text_cfg((void *)desc);
614: 	case GF_ODF_TX3G_TAG:
615: 		return gf_odf_del_tx3g((void *)desc);
616: 	case GF_ODF_ELEM_MASK_TAG:
617: 		return gf_odf_del_ElemMask((void *)desc);
618: 	case GF_ODF_LASER_CFG_TAG:
619: 		return gf_odf_del_laser_cfg((void *)desc);
620: 
621: 	case GF_ODF_AUX_VIDEO_DATA:
622: 		return gf_odf_del_auxvid((void *)desc);
623: 
624: 	case GF_ODF_LANG_TAG:
625: 	case GF_ODF_GPAC_LANG:
626: 		return gf_odf_del_lang((void *)desc);
627: 
628: 	case GF_ODF_ESD_INC_TAG:
629: 		return gf_odf_del_esd_inc((void *)desc);
630: 	case GF_ODF_ESD_REF_TAG:
631: 		return gf_odf_del_esd_ref((void *)desc);
632: 
633: #ifndef GPAC_MINIMAL_ODF
634: 	case GF_ODF_MEDIATIME_TAG:
635: 		return gf_odf_del_mediatime((void *) desc);
636: 	case GF_ODF_CC_TAG:
637: 		return gf_odf_del_cc((void *)desc);
638: 	case GF_ODF_CC_DATE_TAG:
639: 		return gf_odf_del_cc_date((void *)desc);
640: 	case GF_ODF_CC_NAME_TAG:
641: 		return gf_odf_del_cc_name((void *)desc);
642: 	case GF_ODF_CI_TAG:
643: 		return gf_odf_del_ci((void *)desc);
644: 	case GF_ODF_TEXT_TAG:
645: 		return gf_odf_del_exp_text((void *)desc);
646: 	case GF_ODF_EXT_PL_TAG:
647: 		return gf_odf_del_pl_ext((void *)desc);
648: 	case GF_ODF_IPI_PTR_TAG:
649: 	case GF_ODF_ISOM_IPI_PTR_TAG:
650: 		return gf_odf_del_ipi_ptr((void *)desc);
651: 	case GF_ODF_IPMP_TAG:
652: 		return gf_odf_del_ipmp((void *)desc);
653: 	case GF_ODF_IPMP_PTR_TAG:
654: 		return gf_odf_del_ipmp_ptr((void *)desc);
655: 	case GF_ODF_KW_TAG:
656: 		return gf_odf_del_kw((void *)desc);
657: 	case GF_ODF_OCI_DATE_TAG:
658: 		return gf_odf_del_oci_date((void *)desc);
659: 	case GF_ODF_OCI_NAME_TAG:
660: 		return gf_odf_del_oci_name((void *)desc);
661: 	case GF_ODF_PL_IDX_TAG:
662: 		return gf_odf_del_pl_idx((void *)desc);
663: 	case GF_ODF_QOS_TAG:
664: 		return gf_odf_del_qos((void *)desc);
665: 	case GF_ODF_RATING_TAG:
666: 		return gf_odf_del_rating((void *)desc);
667: 	case GF_ODF_REG_TAG:
668: 		return gf_odf_del_reg((void *)desc);
669: 	case GF_ODF_SHORT_TEXT_TAG:
670: 		return gf_odf_del_short_text((void *)desc);
671: 	case GF_ODF_SMPTE_TAG:
672: 		return gf_odf_del_smpte_camera((void *)desc);
673: 	case GF_ODF_SCI_TAG:
674: 		return gf_odf_del_sup_cid((void *)desc);
675: 
676: 	case GF_ODF_IPMP_TL_TAG:
677: 		return gf_odf_del_ipmp_tool_list((void *)desc);
678: 	case GF_ODF_IPMP_TOOL_TAG:
679: 		return gf_odf_del_ipmp_tool((void *)desc);
680: 
681: #endif /*GPAC_MINIMAL_ODF*/
682: 
683: 	default:
684: 		return gf_odf_del_default((void *)desc);
685: 	}
686: 	return GF_OK;
687: }

689: GF_EXPORT
690: void gf_odf_desc_del(GF_Descriptor *desc)
691: {
692: 	if (desc) gf_odf_delete_descriptor(desc);
693: }
694: 
695: static void xmt_node_end(void *sax_cbck, const char *name, const char *name_space)
696: {
697: 	u32 tag;
698: 	GF_XMTParser *parser = (GF_XMTParser *)sax_cbck;
699: 	XMTNodeStack *top;
700: 	GF_Descriptor *desc;
701: 	GF_Node *node = NULL;
702: 	if (!parser->doc_type || !parser->state) return;
703: 
704: 	top = (XMTNodeStack *)gf_list_last(parser->nodes);
705: 
706: 	if (!top) {
707: 		/*check descr*/
708: 		desc = (GF_Descriptor*)gf_list_last(parser->descriptors);
709: 		if (desc && (desc->tag == gf_odf_get_tag_by_name((char *)name)) ) {
710: 
711: 			/*assign timescales once the ESD has been parsed*/
712: 			if (desc->tag == GF_ODF_ESD_TAG) {
713: 				GF_ESD *esd = (GF_ESD*)desc;
714: 				GF_StreamContext *sc = gf_sm_stream_new(parser->load->ctx, esd->ESID, esd->decoderConfig ? esd->decoderConfig->streamType : 0, esd->decoderConfig ? esd->decoderConfig->objectTypeIndication : 0);
715: 				if (sc && esd->slConfig && esd->slConfig->timestampResolution)
716: 					sc->timeScale = esd->slConfig->timestampResolution;
717: 			}
718: 
719: 			gf_list_rem_last(parser->descriptors);
720: 			if (gf_list_count(parser->descriptors)) return;
721: 
722: 			if ((parser->doc_type==1) && (parser->state==XMT_STATE_HEAD) && parser->load->ctx && !parser->load->ctx->root_od) {
723: 				parser->load->ctx->root_od = (GF_ObjectDescriptor *)desc;
724: 			}
725: 			else if (!parser->od_command) {
726: 				xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: descriptor %s defined outside scene scope - skipping", name);
727: 				gf_odf_desc_del(desc);
728: 			} else {
729: 				switch (parser->od_command->tag) {
730: 				case GF_ODF_ESD_UPDATE_TAG:
731: 					gf_list_add( ((GF_ESDUpdate *)parser->od_command)->ESDescriptors, desc);
732: 					break;
733: 				/*same struct for OD update and IPMP update*/
734: 				case GF_ODF_OD_UPDATE_TAG:
735: 				case GF_ODF_IPMP_UPDATE_TAG:
736: 					gf_list_add( ((GF_ODUpdate *)parser->od_command)->objectDescriptors, desc);
737: 					break;
738: 				}
739: 			}
740: 
741: 			return;
742: 		}
743: 		if (parser->state == XMT_STATE_HEAD) {
744: 			if ((parser->doc_type == 1) && !strcmp(name, "Header")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY;
745: 			/*X3D header*/
746: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 2) && !strcmp(name, "head")) {
747: 				parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY;
748: 				/*create a group at root level*/
749: 				tag = xmt_get_node_tag(parser, "Group");
750: 				node = gf_node_new(parser->load->scene_graph, tag);
751: 				gf_node_register(node, NULL);
752: 				gf_sg_set_root_node(parser->load->scene_graph, node);
753: 				gf_node_init(node);
754: 
755: 				/*create a default top for X3D*/
756: 				GF_SAFEALLOC(parser->x3d_root, XMTNodeStack);
757: 				if (!parser->x3d_root) {
758: 					GF_LOG(GF_LOG_ERROR, GF_LOG_PARSER, ("Failed to allocate X3D root\n"));
759: 					return;
760: 				}
761: 				parser->x3d_root->node = node;
762: 			}
763: 			/*XMT-O header*/
764: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 3) && !strcmp(name, "head")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY;
765: 		}
766: 		else if (parser->state == XMT_STATE_ELEMENTS) {
767: 			gf_assert((parser->doc_type != 1) || parser->command);
768: 			if (!strcmp(name, "Replace") || !strcmp(name, "Insert") || !strcmp(name, "Delete")) {
769: 				parser->command = NULL;
770: 				parser->state = XMT_STATE_COMMANDS;
771: 			}
772: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "repField")) {
773: 				parser->state = XMT_STATE_COMMANDS;
774: 			}
775: 			/*end proto*/
776: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "ProtoDeclare") || !strcmp(name, "ExternProtoDeclare"))  {
777: 				GF_Proto *cur = parser->parsing_proto;
778: 				xmt_resolve_routes(parser);
779: 				parser->parsing_proto = (GF_Proto*)cur->userpriv;
780: 				parser->load->scene_graph = cur->parent_graph;
781: 				cur->userpriv = NULL;
782: 			}
783: 			else if (parser->proto_field && !strcmp(name, "field")) parser->proto_field = NULL;
784: 			/*end X3D body*/
785: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 2) && !strcmp(name, "Scene")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY_END;
786: 		}
787: 		else if (parser->state == XMT_STATE_COMMANDS) {
788: 			/*end XMT-A body*/
789: 			if ((parser->doc_type == 1) && !strcmp(name, "Body")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY_END;
790: 			/*end X3D body*/
791: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 2) && !strcmp(name, "Scene")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY_END;
792: 			/*end XMT-O body*/
793: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 3) && !strcmp(name, "body")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_BODY_END;
794: 
795: 			/*end scene command*/
796: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "Replace") || !strcmp(name, "Insert") || !strcmp(name, "Delete") )  {
797: 				/*restore parent command if in CommandBuffer*/
798: 				if (parser->command && parser->command_buffer && parser->command_buffer->buffer) {
799: 					//empty <Insert>
800: 					if ((parser->command->tag==GF_SG_ROUTE_INSERT) && !parser->command->fromNodeID) {
801: 						gf_list_del_item(parser->command_buffer->commandList, parser->command);
802: 					}
803: 
804: 					parser->command = (GF_Command*) parser->command_buffer->buffer;
805: 					parser->command_buffer->buffer = NULL;
806: 					parser->command_buffer = NULL;
807: 				} else {
808: 					//empty <Insert>
809: 					if (parser->command && (parser->command->tag==GF_SG_ROUTE_INSERT) && !parser->command->fromNodeID) {
810: 						gf_list_del_item(parser->scene_au->commands, parser->command);
811: 					}
812: 					parser->command = NULL;
813: 				}
814: 			}
815: 			/*end OD command*/
816: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "ObjectDescriptorUpdate") || !strcmp(name, "ObjectDescriptorRemove")
817: 			         || !strcmp(name, "ES_DescriptorUpdate") || !strcmp(name, "ES_DescriptorRemove")
818: 			         || !strcmp(name, "IPMP_DescriptorUpdate") || !strcmp(name, "IPMP_DescriptorRemove") ) {
819: 				parser->od_command = NULL;
820: 			}
821: 
822: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "par"))
823: 				parser->in_com = 1;
824: 
825: 
826: 		}
827: 		else if (parser->state == XMT_STATE_BODY_END) {
828: 			/*end XMT-A*/
829: 			if ((parser->doc_type == 1) && !strcmp(name, "XMT-A")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_END;
830: 			/*end X3D*/
831: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 2) && !strcmp(name, "X3D")) {
832: 				while (1) {
833: 					GF_Node *n = (GF_Node *)gf_list_last(parser->script_to_load);
834: 					if (!n) break;
835: 					gf_list_rem_last(parser->script_to_load);
836: 					gf_sg_script_load(n);
837: 				}
838: 				gf_list_del(parser->script_to_load);
839: 				parser->script_to_load = NULL;
840: 				parser->state = XMT_STATE_END;
841: 			}
842: 			/*end XMT-O*/
843: 			else if ((parser->doc_type == 3) && !strcmp(name, "XMT-O")) parser->state = XMT_STATE_END;
844: 		}
845: 		return;
846: 	}
847: 	/*only remove created nodes ... */
848: 	tag = xmt_get_node_tag(parser, name);
849: 	if (!tag) {
850: 		if (top->container_field.name) {
851: 			if (!strcmp(name, top->container_field.name)) {
852: 				if (top->container_field.fieldType==GF_SG_VRML_SFCOMMANDBUFFER) {
853: 					parser->state = XMT_STATE_ELEMENTS;
854: 					parser->command = (GF_Command *) (void *) parser->command_buffer->buffer;
855: 					parser->command_buffer->buffer = NULL;
856: 					parser->command_buffer = NULL;
857: 				}
858: 				top->container_field.far_ptr = NULL;
859: 				top->container_field.name = NULL;
860: 				top->last = NULL;
861: 			}
862: 			/*end of command inside an command (conditional.buffer replace)*/
863: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "Replace") || !strcmp(name, "Insert") || !strcmp(name, "Delete") )  {
864: 				if (parser->command_buffer) {
865: 					if (parser->command_buffer->bufferSize) {
866: 						parser->command_buffer->bufferSize--;
867: 					} else {
868: 						SFCommandBuffer *prev = (SFCommandBuffer *) parser->command_buffer->buffer;
869: 						parser->command_buffer->buffer = NULL;
870: 						parser->command_buffer = prev;
871: 					}
872: 					/*stay in command parsing mode (state 3) until we find </buffer>*/
873: 					parser->state = XMT_STATE_COMMANDS;
874: 				}
875: 			}
876: 			/*end of protofield node(s) content*/
877: 			else if (!strcmp(name, "node") || !strcmp(name, "nodes")) {
878: 				top->container_field.far_ptr = NULL;
879: 				top->container_field.name = NULL;
880: 				top->last = NULL;
881: 			}
882: 		}
883: 		/*SF/MFNode proto field, just pop node stack*/
884: 		else if (!top->node && !strcmp(name, "field")) {
885: 			gf_list_rem_last(parser->nodes);
886: 			gf_free(top);
887: 		} else if (top->node && top->node->sgprivate->tag == TAG_ProtoNode) {
888: 			if (!strcmp(name, "node") || !strcmp(name, "nodes")) {
889: 				top->container_field.far_ptr = NULL;
890: 				top->container_field.name = NULL;
891: 				top->last = NULL;
892: 			} else if (!strcmp(name, "ProtoInstance")) {
893: 				gf_list_rem_last(parser->nodes);
894: 				node = top->node;
895: 				gf_free(top);
896: 				goto attach_node;
897: 			}
898: 		}
899: 	} else if (top->node->sgprivate->tag==tag) {
900: 		node = top->node;
901: 		gf_list_rem_last(parser->nodes);
902: 		gf_free(top);
903: 
904: attach_node:
905: 		top = (XMTNodeStack*)gf_list_last(parser->nodes);
906: 		/*add node to command*/
907: 		if (!top || (top->container_field.fieldType==GF_SG_VRML_SFCOMMANDBUFFER)) {
908: 			if (parser->doc_type == 1) {
909: 				GF_CommandField *inf;
910: 				Bool single_node = 0;
911: 				if (!parser->command) {
912: 					gf_assert(0);
913: 					return;
914: 				}
915: 				switch (parser->command->tag) {
916: 				case GF_SG_SCENE_REPLACE:
917: 					if (parser->parsing_proto) {
918: 						gf_sg_proto_add_node_code(parser->parsing_proto, node);
919: 						gf_node_register(node, NULL);
920: 					} else if (!parser->command->node) {
921: 						parser->command->node = node;
922: 						gf_node_register(node, NULL);
923: 					} else if (parser->command->node != node) {
924: 						xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: top-node already assigned - discarding node %s", name);
925: 						gf_node_register(node, NULL);
926: 						gf_node_unregister(node, NULL);
927: 					}
928: 					break;
929: 				case GF_SG_GLOBAL_QUANTIZER:
930: 				case GF_SG_NODE_INSERT:
931: 				case GF_SG_INDEXED_INSERT:
932: 				case GF_SG_INDEXED_REPLACE:
933: 					single_node = 1;
934: 				case GF_SG_NODE_REPLACE:
935: 				case GF_SG_FIELD_REPLACE:
936: 				case GF_SG_MULTIPLE_REPLACE:
937: 					inf = (GF_CommandField*)gf_list_last(parser->command->command_fields);
938: 					if (!inf) {
939: 						inf = gf_sg_command_field_new(parser->command);
940: 						inf->fieldType = GF_SG_VRML_SFNODE;
941: 					}
942: 					if ((inf->fieldType==GF_SG_VRML_MFNODE) && !inf->node_list) {
943: 						inf->field_ptr = &inf->node_list;
944: 						if (inf->new_node) {
945: 							gf_node_list_add_child(& inf->node_list, inf->new_node);
946: 							inf->new_node = NULL;
947: 						}
948: 					}
949: 
950: 					if (inf->new_node) {
951: 						if (single_node) {
952: 							gf_node_unregister(inf->new_node, NULL);
953: 						} else {
954: 							inf->field_ptr = &inf->node_list;
955: 							gf_node_list_add_child(& inf->node_list, inf->new_node);
956: 							inf->fieldType = GF_SG_VRML_MFNODE;
957: 						}
958: 						inf->new_node = NULL;
959: 					}
960: 					gf_node_register(node, NULL);
961: 					if (inf->node_list) {
962: 						gf_node_list_add_child(& inf->node_list, node);
963: 					} else {
964: 						inf->new_node = node;
965: 						inf->field_ptr = &inf->new_node;
966: 					}
967: 					break;
968: 				case GF_SG_PROTO_INSERT:
969: 					if (parser->parsing_proto) {
970: 						gf_sg_proto_add_node_code(parser->parsing_proto, node);
971: 						gf_node_register(node, NULL);
972: 						break;
973: 					}
974: 				default:
975: 					xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: node %s defined outside scene scope - skipping", name);
976: 					gf_node_register(node, NULL);
977: 					gf_node_unregister(node, NULL);
978: 					break;
979: 
980: 				}
981: 			}
982: 			/*X3D*/
983: 			else if (parser->doc_type == 2) {
984: 				if (parser->parsing_proto) {
985: 					gf_sg_proto_add_node_code(parser->parsing_proto, node);
986: 					gf_node_register(node, NULL);
987: 				} else {
988: 					M_Group *gr = (M_Group *)gf_sg_get_root_node(parser->load->scene_graph);
989: 					if (!gr) {
990: 						xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: node %s defined outside scene scope - skipping", name);
991: 						gf_node_register(node, NULL);
992: 						gf_node_unregister(node, NULL);
993: 					} else {
994: 						//node has already been added to its parent with X3d parsing, because of the default container resolving
995: //						gf_node_list_add_child(& gr->children, node);
996: //						gf_node_register(node, NULL);
997: 					}
998: 				}
999: 			}
1000: 			/*special case: replace scene has already been applied (progressive loading)*/
1001: 			else if ((parser->load->flags & GF_SM_LOAD_FOR_PLAYBACK) && (parser->load->scene_graph->RootNode!=node) ) {
1002: 				gf_node_register(node, NULL);
1003: 			} else {
1004: 				xmt_report(parser, GF_OK, "Warning: node %s defined outside scene scope - skipping", name);
1005: 				gf_node_register(node, NULL);
1006: 				gf_node_unregister(node, NULL);
1007: 			}
1008: 		}
1009: 		if (parser->load->flags & GF_SM_LOAD_FOR_PLAYBACK) {
1010: 			/*load scripts*/
1011: 			if (!parser->parsing_proto) {
1012: 				if ((tag==TAG_MPEG4_Script)
1013: #ifndef GPAC_DISABLE_X3D
1014: 				        || (tag==TAG_X3D_Script)
1015: #endif
1016: 				   ) {
1017: 					/*it may happen that the script uses itself as a field (not sure this is compliant since this
1018: 					implies a cyclic structure, but happens in some X3D conformance seq)*/
1019: 					if (!top || (top->node != node)) {
1020: 						if (parser->command) {
1021: 							if (!parser->command->scripts_to_load) parser->command->scripts_to_load = gf_list_new();
1022: 							gf_list_add(parser->command->scripts_to_load, node);
1023: 						}
1024: 						/*do not load script until all routes are established!!*/
1025: 						else if (parser->doc_type==2) {
1026: 							gf_list_add(parser->script_to_load, node);
1027: 						} else {
1028: 							gf_sg_script_load(node);
1029: 						}
1030: 					}
1031: 				}
1032: 			}
1033: 		}
1034: 	} else if (parser->current_node_tag==tag) {
1035: 		gf_list_rem_last(parser->nodes);
1036: 		gf_free(top);
1037: 	} else {
1038: 		xmt_report(parser, GF_NON_COMPLIANT_BITSTREAM, "Warning: closing element %s doesn't match created node %s", name, gf_node_get_class_name(top->node) );
1039: 	}

1042: static void xml_sax_node_end(GF_SAXParser *parser, Bool had_children)
1043: {
1044: 	char *name, c;
1045: 
1046: 	gf_assert(parser->elt_name_start);
1047: 	gf_assert(parser->elt_name_end);
1048: 	if (!parser->node_depth) {
1049: 		format_sax_error(parser, 0, "Markup error");
1050: 		return;
1051: 	}
1052: 	c = parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end - 1];
1053: 	parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end - 1] = 0;
1054: 	name = parser->buffer + parser->elt_name_start - 1;
1055: 
1056: 	if (parser->sax_node_end) {
1057: 		char *sep = strchr(name, ':');
1058: 		if (sep) {
1059: 			sep[0] = 0;
1060: 			parser->sax_node_end(parser->sax_cbck, sep+1, name);
1061: 			sep[0] = ':';
1062: 		} else {
1063: 			parser->sax_node_end(parser->sax_cbck, name, NULL);
1064: 		}
1065: 	}
1066: 	parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end - 1] = c;
1067: 	parser->node_depth--;
1068: 	if (!parser->init_state && !parser->node_depth && parser->sax_state<SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR) parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_DONE;
1069: 	xml_sax_swap(parser);
1070: 	parser->text_start = parser->text_end = 0;
1071: }

1073: static GF_Err xml_sax_parse(GF_SAXParser *parser, Bool force_parse)
1074: {
1075: 	u32 i = 0;
1076: 	Bool is_text;
1077: 	u32 is_end;
1078: 	u8 c;
1079: 	char *elt, sep;
1080: 	u32 cdata_sep;
1081: 
1082: 	while (parser->current_pos<parser->line_size) {
1083: 		if (!force_parse && parser->suspended) goto exit;
1084: 
1085: restart:
1086: 		is_text = GF_FALSE;
1087: 		switch (parser->sax_state) {
1088: 		/*load an XML element*/
1089: 		case SAX_STATE_TEXT_CONTENT:
1090: 			is_text = GF_TRUE;
1091: 		case SAX_STATE_ELEMENT:
1092: 			elt = NULL;
1093: 			i=0;
1094: 			while ((c = parser->buffer[parser->current_pos+i]) !='<') {
1095: 				if ((parser->init_state==2) && (c ==']')) {
1096: 					parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_ATT_NAME;
1097: 					parser->current_pos+=i+1;
1098: 					goto restart;
1099: 				}
1100: 				i++;
1101: 				if (c=='\n') parser->line++;
1102: 				if (is_text) {
1103: 					if (c=='&') parser->text_check_escapes |= 1;
1104: 					else if (c==';') parser->text_check_escapes |= 2;
1105: 				}
1106: 
1107: 				if (parser->current_pos+i==parser->line_size) {
1108: 					if ((parser->line_size >= XML_MAX_CONTENT_SIZE) && !parser->init_state) {
1109: 						GF_LOG(GF_LOG_ERROR, GF_LOG_CORE, ("[XML] Content size larger than max allowed %u, try increasing limit using `-xml-max-csize`\n", XML_MAX_CONTENT_SIZE));
1110: 						parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR;
1111: 					}
1112: 
1113: 					goto exit;
1114: 				}
1115: 			}
1116: 			if (is_text && i) {
1117: 				u32 has_esc = parser->text_check_escapes;
1118: 				xml_sax_store_text(parser, i);
1119: 				parser->text_check_escapes = has_esc;
1120: 				parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_ELEMENT;
1121: 			} else if (i) {
1122: 				parser->current_pos += i;
1123: 				gf_assert(parser->current_pos < parser->line_size);
1124: 			}
1125: 			is_end = 0;
1126: 			i = 0;
1127: 			cdata_sep = 0;
1128: 			while (1) {
1129: 				c = parser->buffer[parser->current_pos+1+i];
1130: 				if (!strncmp(parser->buffer+parser->current_pos+1+i, "!--", 3)) {
1131: 					parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_COMMENT;
1132: 					i += 3;
1133: 					break;
1134: 				}
1135: 				if (!c) {
1136: 					goto exit;
1137: 				}
1138: 				if ((c=='\t') || (c=='\r') || (c==' ') ) {
1139: 					if (i) break;
1140: 					else parser->current_pos++;
1141: 				}
1142: 				else if (c=='\n') {
1143: 					parser->line++;
1144: 					if (i) break;
1145: 					else parser->current_pos++;
1146: 				}
1147: 				else if (c=='>') break;
1148: 				else if (c=='=') break;
1149: 				else if (c=='[') {
1150: 					i++;
1151: 					if (!cdata_sep) cdata_sep = 1;
1152: 					else {
1153: 						break;
1154: 					}
1155: 				}
1156: 				else if (c=='/') {
1157: 					is_end = !i ? 1 : 2;
1158: 					i++;
1159: 				} else if (c=='<') {
1160: 					if (parser->sax_state != SAX_STATE_COMMENT) {
1161: 						parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR;
1162: 						return GF_CORRUPTED_DATA;
1163: 					}
1164: 				} else {
1165: 					i++;
1166: 				}
1167: 				/*				if ((c=='[') && (parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_start-1 + i-2]=='A') ) break; */
1168: 				if (parser->current_pos+1+i==parser->line_size) {
1169: 					goto exit;
1170: 				}
1171: 			}
1172: 			if (i) {
1173: 				parser->elt_name_start = parser->current_pos+1 + 1;
1174: 				if (is_end==1) parser->elt_name_start ++;
1175: 				if (is_end==2) parser->elt_name_end = parser->current_pos+1+i;
1176: 				else parser->elt_name_end = parser->current_pos+1+i + 1;
1177: 			}
1178: 			if (is_end) {
1179: 				xml_sax_flush_text(parser);
1180: 				parser->elt_end_pos = parser->file_pos + parser->current_pos + i;
1181: 				if (is_end==2) {
1182: 					parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_ELEMENT;
1183: 					xml_sax_node_start(parser);
1184: 					xml_sax_node_end(parser, GF_FALSE);
1185: 				} else {
1186: 					parser->elt_end_pos += parser->elt_name_end - parser->elt_name_start;
1187: 					xml_sax_node_end(parser, GF_TRUE);
1188: 				}
1189: 				if (parser->sax_state == SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR) break;
1190: 				parser->current_pos+=2+i;
1191: 				parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_TEXT_CONTENT;
1192: 				break;
1193: 			}
1194: 			if (!parser->elt_name_end) {
1195: 				return GF_CORRUPTED_DATA;
1196: 			}
1197: 			sep = parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end-1];
1198: 			parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end-1] = 0;
1199: 			elt = parser->buffer + parser->elt_name_start-1;
1200: 
1201: 			parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_ATT_NAME;
1202: 			gf_assert(parser->elt_start_pos <= parser->file_pos + parser->current_pos);
1203: 			parser->elt_start_pos = parser->file_pos + parser->current_pos;
1204: 
1205: 			if (!strncmp(elt, "!--", 3)) {
1206: 				xml_sax_flush_text(parser);
1207: 				parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_COMMENT;
1208: 				if (i>3) parser->current_pos -= (i-3);
1209: 			}
1210: 			else if (!strcmp(elt, "?xml")) parser->init_state = 1;
1211: 			else if (!strcmp(elt, "!DOCTYPE")) parser->init_state = 2;
1212: 			else if (!strcmp(elt, "!ENTITY")) parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_ENTITY;
1213: 			else if (!strcmp(elt, "!ATTLIST") || !strcmp(elt, "!ELEMENT")) parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_SKIP_DOCTYPE;
1214: 			else if (!strcmp(elt, "![CDATA["))
1215: 				parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_CDATA;
1216: 			else if (elt[0]=='?') {
1217: 				i--;
1218: 				parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_XML_PROC;
1219: 			}
1220: 			/*node found*/
1221: 			else {
1222: 				xml_sax_flush_text(parser);
1223: 				if (parser->init_state) {
1224: 					parser->init_state = 0;
1225: 					/*that's a bit ugly: since we solve entities when appending text, we need to
1226: 					reparse the current buffer*/
1227: 					if (gf_list_count(parser->entities)) {
1228: 						char *orig_buf;
1229: 						GF_Err e;
1230: 						parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end-1] = sep;
1231: 						orig_buf = gf_strdup(parser->buffer + parser->current_pos);
1232: 						parser->current_pos = 0;
1233: 						parser->line_size = 0;
1234: 						parser->elt_start_pos = 0;
1235: 						parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_TEXT_CONTENT;
1236: 						parser->ent_rec_level++;
1237: 						if (parser->ent_rec_level>100) {
1238: 							GF_LOG(GF_LOG_WARNING, GF_LOG_CORE, ("[XML] Too many recursions in entity solving, max 100 allowed\n"));
1239: 							e = GF_NOT_SUPPORTED;
1240: 						} else {
1241: 							e = gf_xml_sax_parse_intern(parser, orig_buf);
1242: 							parser->ent_rec_level--;
1243: 						}
1244: 						gf_free(orig_buf);
1245: 						return e;
1246: 					}
1247: 				}
1248: 			}
1249: 			parser->current_pos+=1+i;
1250: 			parser->buffer[parser->elt_name_end-1] = sep;
1251: 			break;
1252: 		case SAX_STATE_COMMENT:
1253: 			if (!xml_sax_parse_comments(parser)) {
1254: 				xml_sax_swap(parser);
1255: 				goto exit;
1256: 			}
1257: 			break;
1258: 		case SAX_STATE_ATT_NAME:
1259: 		case SAX_STATE_ATT_VALUE:
1260: 			if (xml_sax_parse_attribute(parser))
1261: 				goto exit;
1262: 			break;
1263: 		case SAX_STATE_ENTITY:
1264: 			xml_sax_parse_entity(parser);
1265: 			break;
1266: 		case SAX_STATE_SKIP_DOCTYPE:
1267: 			xml_sax_skip_doctype(parser);
1268: 			break;
1269: 		case SAX_STATE_XML_PROC:
1270: 			xml_sax_skip_xml_proc(parser);
1271: 			break;
1272: 		case SAX_STATE_CDATA:

1274: 			break;
1275: 		case SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR:

1277: 		case SAX_STATE_ALLOC_ERROR:

1279: 		case SAX_STATE_DONE:
1280: 			return GF_EOS;
1281: 		}
1282: 	}
1283: exit:
1284: #if 0
1285: 	if (is_text) {

1292: 	if (parser->sax_state==SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR)
1293: 		return GF_CORRUPTED_DATA;

1298: static GF_Err gf_xml_sax_parse_intern(GF_SAXParser *parser, char *current)
1299: {
1300: 	u32 count;
1301: 	/*solve entities*/
1302: 	count = gf_list_count(parser->entities);
1303: 	while (count) {
1304: 		char *entityEnd;
1305: 		XML_Entity *ent;
1306: 		char *entityStart = strstr(current, "&");
1307: 		Bool needs_text;
1308: 		u32 line_num;
1309: 
1310: 		/*if in entity, the start of the entity is in the buffer !!*/
1311: 		if (parser->in_entity) {
1312: 			u32 len;
1313: 			char *name;
1314: 			entityEnd = strstr(current, ";");
1315: 			if (!entityEnd) return xml_sax_append_string(parser, current);
1316: 			entityStart = strrchr(parser->buffer, '&');
1317: 
1318: 			entityEnd[0] = 0;
1319: 			len = (u32) strlen(entityStart) + (u32) strlen(current) + 1;
1320: 			name = (char*)gf_malloc(sizeof(char)*len);
1321: 			sprintf(name, "%s%s;", entityStart+1, current);
1322: 
1323: 			ent = gf_xml_locate_entity(parser, name, &needs_text);
1324: 			gf_free(name);
1325: 
1326: 			//entity not found, parse as regular string
1327: 			if (!ent && !needs_text) {
1328: 				xml_sax_append_string(parser, current);
1329: 				xml_sax_parse(parser, GF_TRUE);
1330: 				entityEnd[0] = ';';
1331: 				current = entityEnd;
1332: 				parser->in_entity = GF_FALSE;
1333: 				continue;
1334: 			}
1335: 			gf_assert(ent);
1336: 			/*truncate input buffer*/
1337: 			parser->line_size -= (u32) strlen(entityStart);
1338: 			entityStart[0] = 0;
1339: 
1340: 			parser->in_entity = GF_FALSE;
1341: 			entityEnd[0] = ';';
1342: 			current = entityEnd+1;
1343: 		} else {
1344: 			if (!entityStart) break;
1345: 
1346: 			ent = gf_xml_locate_entity(parser, entityStart+1, &needs_text);
1347: 
1348: 			/*store current string before entity start*/
1349: 			entityStart[0] = 0;
1350: 			xml_sax_append_string(parser, current);
1351: 			xml_sax_parse(parser, GF_TRUE);
1352: 			entityStart[0] = '&';
1353: 
1354: 			/*this is not an entitiy*/
1355: 			if (!ent && !needs_text) {
1356: 				xml_sax_append_string(parser, "&");
1357: 				current = entityStart+1;
1358: 				continue;
1359: 			}
1360: 
1361: 			if (!ent) {
1362: 				parser->in_entity = GF_TRUE;
1363: 				/*store entity start*/
1364: 				return xml_sax_append_string(parser, entityStart);
1365: 			}
1366: 			current = entityStart + ent->namelen + 2;
1367: 		}
1368: 		/*append entity*/
1369: 		line_num = parser->line;
1370: 		xml_sax_append_string(parser, ent->value);
1371: 		GF_Err e = xml_sax_parse(parser, GF_TRUE);
1372: 		parser->line = line_num;
1373: 		if (e) return e;
1374: 
1375: 	}
1376: 	xml_sax_append_string(parser, current);
1377: 	return xml_sax_parse(parser, GF_FALSE);
1378: }

1380: GF_EXPORT
1381: GF_Err gf_xml_sax_parse(GF_SAXParser *parser, const void *string)
1382: {
1383: 	GF_Err e;
1384: 	char *current;
1385: 	char *utf_conv = NULL;
1386: 
1387: 	if (parser->unicode_type < 0) return GF_BAD_PARAM;
1388: 
1389: 	if (parser->unicode_type>1) {
1390: 		const u16 *sptr = (const u16 *)string;
1391: 		u32 len = 2 * gf_utf8_wcslen(sptr);
1392: 		utf_conv = (char *)gf_malloc(sizeof(char)*(len+1));
1393: 		len = gf_utf8_wcstombs(utf_conv, len, &sptr);
1394: 		if (len == GF_UTF8_FAIL) {
1395: 			parser->sax_state = SAX_STATE_SYNTAX_ERROR;
1396: 			gf_free(utf_conv);
1397: 			return GF_CORRUPTED_DATA;
1398: 		}
1399: 		utf_conv[len] = 0;
1400: 		current = utf_conv;
1401: 	} else {
1402: 		current = (char *)string;
1403: 	}
1404: 
1405: 	e = gf_xml_sax_parse_intern(parser, current);
1406: 	if (utf_conv) gf_free(utf_conv);
1407: 	return e;
1408: }

1440: 	if (gf_gzeof(parser->gz_in)) {
1441: #endif

1443: 		if (parser->on_progress) parser->on_progress(parser->sax_cbck, parser->file_size, parser->file_size);
1444: 
1445: #ifdef NO_GZIP
1446: 		gf_fclose(parser->f_in);
1447: 		parser->f_in = NULL;
1448: #else
1449: 		gf_gzclose(parser->gz_in);
1450: 		parser->gz_in = 0;
1451: #endif
1452: 
1453: 		parser->elt_start_pos = parser->elt_end_pos = 0;
1454: 		parser->elt_name_start = parser->elt_name_end = 0;
1455: 		parser->att_name_start = 0;
1456: 		parser->current_pos = 0;
1457: 		parser->line_size = 0;
1458: 		parser->att_sep = 0;
1459: 		parser->file_pos = 0;
1460: 		parser->file_size = 0;
1461: 		parser->line_size = 0;
1462: 	}
1463: 	return e;
1464: }

499: GF_EXPORT
500: GF_SAXParser *gf_xml_sax_new(gf_xml_sax_node_start on_node_start,
501:                               gf_xml_sax_node_end on_node_end,
502:                               gf_xml_sax_text_content on_text_content,
503:                               void *cbck)
504: {
505: 	GF_SAXParser *parser;
506: 	GF_SAFEALLOC(parser, GF_SAXParser);
507: 	if (!parser) return NULL;
508: 	parser->entities = gf_list_new();
509: 	parser->unicode_type = -1;
510: 	parser->sax_node_start = on_node_start;
511: 	parser->sax_node_end = on_node_end;
512: 	parser->sax_text_content = on_text_content;
513: 	parser->sax_cbck = cbck;
514: 	return parser;
515: }

517: static GF_XMTParser *xmt_new_parser(GF_SceneLoader *load)
518: {
519: 	GF_XMTParser *parser;
520: 	if ((load->type==GF_SM_LOAD_XSR) && !load->ctx) return NULL;
521: 	GF_SAFEALLOC(parser, GF_XMTParser);
522: 	if (!parser) return NULL;
523: 
524: 	parser->nodes = gf_list_new();
525: 	parser->descriptors = gf_list_new();
526: 	parser->od_links = gf_list_new();
527: 	parser->esd_links = gf_list_new();
528: 	parser->def_nodes = gf_list_new();
529: 	parser->peeked_nodes = gf_list_new();
530: 	parser->inserted_routes = gf_list_new();
531: 	parser->unresolved_routes = gf_list_new();
532: 
533: 	parser->sax_parser = gf_xml_sax_new(xmt_node_start, xmt_node_end, xmt_text_content, parser);
534: 	parser->load = load;
535: 	load->loader_priv = parser;
536: 	if (load->ctx) load->ctx->is_pixel_metrics = 1;
537: 
538: 	return parser;
539: }

541: static GF_Err load_xmt_initialize(GF_SceneLoader *load, const char *str_data)
542: {
543: 	GF_Err e;
544: 	GF_XMTParser *parser;
545: 
546: 	if (str_data) {
547: 		char BOM[5];
548: 		if (strlen(str_data)<4) return GF_BAD_PARAM;
549: 		BOM[0] = str_data[0];
550: 		BOM[1] = str_data[1];
551: 		BOM[2] = str_data[2];
552: 		BOM[3] = str_data[3];
553: 		BOM[4] = 0;
554: 		parser = xmt_new_parser(load);
555: 		e = gf_xml_sax_init(parser->sax_parser, (unsigned char*)BOM);
556: 		if (e) {
557: 			xmt_report(parser, e, "Error initializing SAX parser");
558: 			return e;
559: 		}
560: 		str_data += 4;
561: 	} else if (load->fileName) {
562: 		parser = xmt_new_parser(load);
563: 	} else {
564: 		return GF_BAD_PARAM;
565: 	}
566: 
567: 	/*chunk parsing*/
568: 	if (load->flags & GF_SM_LOAD_CONTEXT_READY) {
569: 		GF_LOG(GF_LOG_INFO, GF_LOG_PARSER, ("XMT: MPEG-4 (XMT) Chunk Parsing\n"));
570: 
571: 		e = xmt_restore_context(load);
572: 		if (e) return e;
573: 
574: 	} else {
575: 		GF_LOG(GF_LOG_INFO, GF_LOG_PARSER, ("XMT: MPEG-4 (XMT) Scene Parsing\n"));
576: 	}
577: 
578: 	if (str_data) {
579: 		return gf_xml_sax_parse(parser->sax_parser, str_data);
580: 	}
581: 	return GF_OK;
582: }
